title: Jokeasy: Exploring Human-AI Collaboration in Thematic Joke Generation
research_questions: How can human writers and search-enabled LLM agents collaborate effectively throughout the process of thematic joke creation?
How can real-time, web-sourced information be presented and integrated in human–AI co-creation to enrich rather than overwhelm the creative process?
How should responsibilities be divided between the human writer and the AI during ideation, refinement, and prototype generation?
What types of interfaces can best reveal the agent’s reasoning while maintaining user control and creative flow?

datasets_used.name: Jokeasy qualitative user study data
datasets_used.size: 18 participants (P1–P18); 13 general users and 5 expert users

datasets_used.name: Web search results retrieved via Tavily API

methods_proposed.name: Jokeasy
methods_proposed.summary: A search-enabled visual co-writing prototype for thematic joke generation. It lets users gather web material, organize it into editable inspiration blocks, and develop multiple joke maps on a visual canvas with an LLM agent.

methods_proposed.name: Dual-role LLM agent
methods_proposed.summary: The LLM agent acts first as a material scout that searches for timely, relevant material and then as a prototype writer that assembles setup-and-punchline joke skeletons from the retrieved content.

methods_proposed.name: Four-stage inspiration-block workflow
methods_proposed.summary: The workflow consists of topic ideation, inspiration generation and initial prototype creation, inspiration validation and collaborative refinement, and final joke synthesis. Retrieved search results are transformed into manipulable inspiration blocks that users can edit, delete, add, or regenerate.

evaluation_methods.detail: Counter-balanced qualitative user study with 18 participants: 13 general users and 5 expert users individually used a baseline conversational system and Jokeasy for stand-up comedy joke creation tasks, with a suggested 15-minute limit per task. The study collected think-aloud comments, on-screen actions, video recordings, system logs, and 15-minute semi-structured interviews, followed by thematic analysis.

headline_results.finding: Most participants favoured Jokeasy over the baseline chat tool for thematic-joke creation.
Eight participants said the agent reliably matched their intentions, sparked new ideas, or helped when they ran out of ideas.
Fourteen participants praised the canvas for its structured clarity.
All five experts saw clear value in pairing an LLM with live search, especially for humour that must reference current events.
Most experts felt the non-linear canvas fits joke-writing practice.

key_findings: Jokeasy’s structured four-stage workflow and editable visual canvas made thematic joke creation feel more organized and controllable than the baseline conversational system for most participants.
Integrated search and one-to-one links between inspiration blocks and supporting web evidence helped users trace why the AI generated an inspiration and browse source material more efficiently.
Some retrieved results were judged too general, weakly related, redundant, or overly broad, leading users and experts to ask for finer search control and source selection.
Several participants valued the canvas as a mind-map-like collaboration scaffold, but some found it initially complex or less naturally conversational than the baseline.
Experts viewed Jokeasy as especially useful for novices and as a drafting aid for experienced writers, while emphasizing that final comic judgment must remain with human creators.
limitations: The prototype was developed to explore early-stage thematic joke creation and does not yet support comprehensive editing or refinement of complete stand-up scripts.
The implementation relies on a simplified web search tool, which occasionally yielded overly broad or insufficiently specific results.
The qualitative user study had a relatively modest sample size, limiting broader generalizability.
The general-purpose orientation of Jokeasy may have constrained deeper insights tailored to specialized user groups or professional contexts.
safety_ethics_notes: Participants provided informed consent; general users received 40 RMB and expert users received 200 RMB. The paper also notes expert concerns about controversial retrieved items needing user filtering, the need to balance surprise and social appropriateness, and worries that easy AI generation might dilute originality and threaten professional livelihoods.

artifacts.other: Prototype front-end developed as a Figma widget plugin; back-end built using Node.js; search implemented using the Tavily API. No public code or data URL is stated.

human_samples.population: General users P1–P13, described as hobbyists and enthusiasts interested in stand-up/thematic joke writing.
human_samples.recruitment: Recruited via an online questionnaire distributed through social media platforms.
human_samples.observations: Each participant interacted individually with both the baseline conversational system and Jokeasy in counter-balanced order; data collected included pre-test questionnaire responses, think-aloud comments, on-screen actions, video recordings, system logs, and a 15-minute semi-structured interview.
human_samples.detail: All participants were native Chinese speakers, and LLM outputs were configured in Chinese. General users received 40 RMB. Each session lasted approximately one hour; each system task had a suggested 15-minute limit, but no strict time constraint.

human_samples.population: Expert users P14–P18/E1–E5. E1–E3 were comedians with over five stand-up performance experiences; E4 was a human-computer interaction specialist and stand-up enthusiast; E5 was an AI researcher and stand-up enthusiast.
human_samples.observations: Experts interacted with the systems and gave interview-based judgments about practical value, technical feasibility, design implications, search-enabled collaboration, and the visual canvas; reported findings include all five experts seeing value in pairing an LLM with live search and 4/5 experts viewing the non-linear canvas as fitting joke-writing practice.
human_samples.detail: All participants were native Chinese speakers, and LLM outputs were configured in Chinese. Expert users received 200 RMB. Expert interviews focused more on professional humour practice and broader applications than the general-user sessions.

judges.name: General users P1–P13
judges.detail: General participants supplied subjective preferences, think-aloud reactions, and interview feedback after using visibly different systems. The study does not report blinding; the two interfaces were materially different and participants watched tutorials for each.

judges.name: Expert users P14–P18/E1–E5
judges.detail: Expert users supplied judgments about Jokeasy’s value for novices and experienced writers, search-enabled collaboration, technical limits, and canvas interaction. They were not reported as blinded.

judges.name: Paper authors
judges.detail: The authors analyzed recordings, logs, think-aloud comments, and post-session interviews using thematic analysis. The paper does not report coder count, codebook details, inter-rater reliability, or analyst blinding.

generation_selection.scope: Jokeasy default initial joke-map generation after the user confirms a topic summary
generation_selection.n: 3 joke maps by default, derived from three high-level inspiration themes
generation_selection.detail: For each confirmed topic, Jokeasy derives three inspiration themes, expands search keywords, queries the web, distills inspiration blocks, and produces a provisional joke title and prototype joke content for each map. The three maps are shown side by side for user inspection, selection, editing, or further refinement; no automatic ranking is reported.

generation_selection.scope: Jokeasy user-controlled refinement, added maps, and regenerated jokes
generation_selection.n: Variable and user-controlled; in the P7 use case, the participant added one AI joke map, reviewed four maps, selected Joke Map 03, then used Regenerate Joke three times before choosing a preferred output. Counts across all participants are not reported.
generation_selection.detail: Users could delete or edit inspiration blocks, use AI Add or Manual Add, add AI-generated or blank joke maps, and regenerate joke content. Selection was by the human user during the interactive co-writing process.

generation_selection.scope: Baseline conversational system in the user study
generation_selection.n: One response per user prompt/turn as described; users could request additional versions through follow-up prompts, but no fixed number of candidates across participants is reported.
generation_selection.detail: The baseline used the same LLM model and had search capabilities, but lacked Jokeasy’s staged workflow and visual co-creation features. In the P7 baseline use case, the participant requested a second version, asked the system to merge ideas, prompted online search, and then asked to enhance expression.

model_provenance.name: moonshot-v1-auto
model_provenance.detail: The paper states that Jokeasy uses moonshot-v1-auto, supporting up to 128k tokens, and that the baseline conversational system used the same LLM model. No provider endpoint, API access details, snapshot, or inference dates are reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Initial Generate Jokes creates three high-level inspiration themes and presents three joke maps; users may add or remove maps and inspiration blocks, so later candidate counts are variable and not reported.
inference_budgets.token_budget: The model is reported to support up to 128k tokens; per-call input/output token limits are not reported.
inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Temperature parameter set to 0.3; LLM outputs in the study were configured to be in Chinese.
inference_budgets.detail: Front end was a Figma widget plugin; back end used Node.js. LLM functions were implemented using an LLM-chain method and structured output feature. Search used the Tavily API. Figure 3 names modules such as topicSumGen, inspirationThemeGen, jokeGen, queryGen, search4LLM, echoGen, inspirationPopupGen, and newInspirationGen, but exact call counts are not reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Not fixed or fully reported. The paper describes conversational turns and one illustrative P7 sequence, including ten relevant cases returned after a search request, but does not report candidate counts across participants.
inference_budgets.token_budget: Same LLM model as Jokeasy; per-call token limits are not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: The baseline had search capabilities and could integrate retrieved information into its outputs, but lacked Jokeasy’s multi-step search integration, staged workflow, and visual co-creation features. Its exact prompt, search settings, and sampling settings are not specified.

human_baselines.description: No unaided human-only writing condition or expert-authored joke baseline is reported. The study compares Jokeasy against a conversational AI baseline using the same LLM model and search capability.
human_baselines.detail: Human participants were users/evaluators in both AI-assisted conditions, not a separate human baseline whose outputs were evaluated under matched conditions.

contamination_risk.basis: The paper does not use a fixed public benchmark or report model-performance scores on a test set. Its empirical claims are based on live HCI sessions, participant preferences, interviews, think-aloud data, logs, and author thematic analysis of interactions with two systems. Therefore benchmark memorization/contamination is not a meaningful threat to a benchmark result here, although the paper also does not report any decontamination analysis.

llm_judge_dependence.detail: The LLM generated summaries, inspiration blocks, joke maps, echo summaries, and conversational outputs, but it was not used to judge joke quality, label data, or score systems. Headline conclusions rest on participant/expert feedback and author thematic analysis.

reporting_gaps: No exact model provider access method, API endpoint, model snapshot, or inference dates are reported beyond the identifier moonshot-v1-auto.
No exact LLM call counts per topic, per joke map, per regeneration, or per participant are reported.
No per-call maximum input/output token budgets are reported; only the model’s 128k-token support is stated.
Baseline configuration is under-specified: prompt/system prompt, sampling parameters, search workflow, and retrieval integration details are not provided.
Tavily API settings are not reported, including retrieval dates, query limits, result-count defaults, ranking/depth settings, or source filters.
The paper mentions prompt templates and shows examples in figures, but does not provide complete machine-readable prompts for all LLM modules.
The list of creative tasks/topics given to participants and the full counter-balancing schedule are not reported.
The paper does not report participants’ final joke outputs or independent joke-quality ratings.
The thematic-analysis procedure is under-specified: coder count, codebook development, inter-coder reliability, adjudication process, and analyst blinding are not reported.
Participant demographics beyond user category, expertise descriptions, and native Chinese language are limited; participant geography is not reported.
No public code, logs, dataset, or artifact URL is reported.
No inference compute, latency, or monetary cost for LLM/search use is reported.
